Boston Metropolitan Planning Council · Tract Investment Targeting

Two attributes explain most of Boston’s housing-value gap — and neither is pollution or tax

A diagnostic and predictive analysis of 506 census tracts, commissioned to direct a fixed neighbourhood-investment budget.

Prepared 20 July 2026 · Source: Harrison & Rubinfeld (1978), UCI ML Repository · Boston SMSA, 1970 · Analysis: Path A (diagnostic) + Path B (predictive)

Situation
Median home values across Boston’s 506 census tracts span $5k to $50k around a $21.2k median — a tenfold spread — and a fixed investment budget must be allocated across them.
Complication
Structural, socioeconomic, environmental and locational attributes all correlate with value, so raw correlations cannot discriminate between them. Each family implies a completely different lever, and acting on the wrong one wastes the budget.
Resolution
Two attributes dominate, their effects bend sharply, and the remaining factors turn out to be symptoms rather than causes.

Variation in Boston tract home values is driven overwhelmingly by two attributes — social composition and dwelling size — whose effects on value are strongly non-linear; environmental and fiscal factors track value only because they track those two, and are therefore diagnostic markers rather than independent levers.

  1. Two attributes explain most of the spread. Social composition alone accounts for 54% of the variation in tract value and dwelling size for 48%; together with their derivatives they carry 86% of the predictive weight in the final model, against 14% for everything else.
  2. Both relationships bend sharply, so linear models misprice exactly the tracts that matter. Allowing non-linearity lifts explained variance from 81% to 92% — 11.4 points — with the gains concentrated in the poorest and most affluent tracts.
  3. Environmental and fiscal factors are inherited, not independent. After holding social composition and dwelling size constant, air quality retains just 7% of its apparent association with value and industrial land 13%. They mark distressed neighbourhoods; they do not independently set prices.

Recommendation

The Council’s planning team should, within the next budget cycle, commission site assessments of the 18 tracts flagged in Exhibit 6 as valued more than $2,000 below their predicted value — beginning with the $9.0k largest gap — rather than allocating against pollution or tax-burden indicators. Those indicators identify distressed areas but, on this evidence, do not independently move value.

Scope boundary: this analysis establishes association, not causation. It tells the Council where to look first. It cannot say what intervention would work, and no figure in it should be read as an estimate of an intervention’s effect. Establishing that requires a study design this 1970 cross-section cannot support.

92% of value variation explained on held-out tracts
$2.49k typical prediction error, vs $8.93k for a naive baseline
86% of predictive weight held by just two attribute families
18 tracts valued materially below prediction

Part I · What drives value

Key Line 1 — Social composition and dwelling size account for most of the spread

Eleven candidate attributes were tested against median tract value, with Benjamini–Hochberg correction for multiple comparisons. All eleven are statistically significant — which is precisely why significance is the wrong filter. On a sample of 506 tracts, almost anything reaches significance; the question is what is material.

Restated in the units the Council budgets in: moving a tract across the interquartile range of social composition is associated with a $9.51k swing in median value — roughly 45% of the $21.2k median — while dwelling size moves it $6.72k.

Part II · How it drives value

Key Line 2 — The relationships bend, and ignoring that misprices both tails

The association is not a straight line in either case, and the departure is large enough to change which tracts get flagged.

Six model families were compared on repeated 10-fold cross-validation (30 fits each) using training data only; the held-out set was scored once, after the winner was fixed.

ModelCV R²Test R²Test RMSE ($k)Test MAE ($k)RMSE vs baseline
Gradient Boosting0.8520.9222.491.8172%
Random Forest0.8240.8982.851.9068%
OLS0.7610.8083.902.9656%
Lasso0.7610.8063.932.9656%
Ridge0.7640.7993.992.9955%
Baseline (predict mean)-0.030-0.0078.936.310%

A note on the two R² figures

Test R² (0.922) sits above cross-validated R² (0.852). With only 102 held-out tracts that gap is sampling variation, not evidence of a better model. The cross-validated figure is the more trustworthy estimate of real-world performance, and both are reported rather than only the flattering one.

Part III · What does not drive value

Key Line 3 — Environmental and fiscal indicators are symptoms, not levers

Air quality, industrial land share and property-tax rate all correlate with home value at first glance. The question that matters for budgeting is whether they still do once the two dominant attributes are held constant.

One exception is worth naming: river adjacency is the single environmental attribute whose association strengthens under control, making it a genuine and independent — if small — premium of about $6.3k. It rests on 35 tracts with a confidence interval spanning $2.4k to $10.3k, so it is reported here and used nowhere.

Part IV · Where to look

The residuals convert the model into a shortlist

Tracts whose actual value falls well below what their own attributes predict are, by construction, the places where something unmeasured is suppressing value.

Part V · Limitations that bear on the decision

Three constraints the Council should weigh before acting

1. The model is blind to race, not neutral about it

The dataset contains a race-derived column. It was excluded from every model — but exclusion is not a defence, and the audit says so plainly. Removing it changed accuracy by 0.003 R², because the remaining features already carry the information: a model can reconstruct that column from ordinary tract attributes at R² ≈ 0.4, with crime rate the strongest single correlate (|r| = 0.509).

Black population share (recovered)nMAE ($k)Mean bias ($k)Mean actual ($k)
None (Bk = 0)352.03+0.4021.7
Low (0-10%)621.71+0.0524.7
Higher (>10%)51.65+0.4113.5

Consequence for use

This model is fit for ranking tracts on housing and social attributes. It is not fit for allocating resources between demographic groups. The subgroup estimates above are themselves coarse — the smallest group holds just 5 tracts — so they establish that proxy encoding is present and material, not a precise measure of disparate impact.

2. The top of the market is invisible

16 tracts are recorded at exactly $50k, the survey ceiling. Their true values are unknown but higher, so every high-end figure in this report is an attenuated lower bound. Re-running the analysis without them moves explained variance to 87% and leaves error essentially unchanged ($2.55k vs $2.49k) — the conclusions do not turn on the treatment.

3. The data is from 1970 and describes association only

  • No causal claim is supported. A single cross-section has no assignment mechanism, no instrument and no pre/post structure. The recommendation is deliberately framed as targeting, never as intervention sizing.
  • Values are in 1970 dollars and carry no inflation adjustment; the figures are not comparable to present-day prices.
  • Property-tax rate cannot be interpreted separately from highway accessibility — they correlate at 0.91, so no “tax burden” conclusion is identifiable here.
  • No tract identifiers or coordinates exist in the source, so the shortlist is by row index and must be joined to Council records before any site visit.

Appendix · Method

How this was produced

  • Data — 506 census tracts × 13 attributes, Boston SMSA 1970. Zero missing values and zero duplicates, verified rather than assumed; source integrity fixed by SHA-256 at ingestion.
  • Cleaning — no rows dropped, no values imputed. Three structural decisions: censoring flagged, the highway index treated as categorical (it jumps 1–8 then to 24), and the crime-rate tail retained as genuine signal.
  • Diagnostic (Path A) — Pearson and Spearman with Fisher-z confidence intervals, Benjamini–Hochberg FDR across 11 tests, partial correlation to separate inherited from independent association, VIF for collinearity, Welch and Mann–Whitney for the river comparison.
  • Predictive (Path B) — seven engineered features, each traced to a diagnostic finding; all fitted transforms inside a pipeline so nothing leaks across folds; 30 cross-validation fits per model; 102 tracts held out and scored once.
  • Winner — Gradient Boosting, test R² 0.922, RMSE $2.49k, MAE $1.81k, a 72% error reduction against predicting the mean.
  • Reproducibility — fixed seed 42, pinned dependencies, relative paths, five notebooks that run end to end. Every figure quoted above is read from a machine-written file, never retyped.

Boston Housing tract analysis · prepared 20 July 2026 · Tier A deliverable following the Pyramid Principle, MECE structuring and SCR storylining.

Source: Harrison, D. and Rubinfeld, D.L. (1978), “Hedonic prices and the demand for clean air”, Journal of Environmental Economics and Management 5, 81–102, via the UCI Machine Learning Repository. 506 census tracts, Boston SMSA, 1970. Median value top-coded at $50k (16 tracts).

Ethical note: this dataset contains a race-derived variable and is widely criticised for it; scikit-learn deprecated its loader on those grounds. It is used here for methodological demonstration, with that column excluded from modelling and audited explicitly. Findings describe 1970 conditions and carry no present-day policy implication.
